main_summary.summary: Harold K. Brown, born 1934 in York, Pennsylvania, recounts his path from segregated schools in a small Pennsylvania farm community to leadership of the San Diego chapter of the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) in the early 1960s. After Army service at Camp Gordon and Fort Monmouth, NJ, he migrated to San Diego via San Diego State University. He joined the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee and then helped found San Diego CORE around 1961, becoming chapter chairman and later chairman of CORE's Western Region. He led campaigns against employment discrimination at Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric using marches, sit-ins, mail-ins, and coin-ins, serving weekend jail sentences for trespassing while continuing to teach school. He describes housing discrimination affecting his future wife when she arrived to work at General Dynamics in 1967, internal organizational conflict with Trotskyites and peace-movement infiltrators, and his decision to resign from CORE. Brown served as Peace Corps deputy director in Lesotho during apartheid, returning after Martin Luther King's assassination. He founded San Diego State's African American Studies program in 1971, later directed computer services, served as associate dean of the College of Business Administration, and founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Brown's testimony documents the West Coast civil rights movement, an under-recorded geography in the standard Southern-centered narrative. His account of San Diego CORE's tactical innovations (mail-ins and coin-ins against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric) preserves a regional direct-action vocabulary that supplements the better-known Southern sit-in tradition. The interview also captures internal-organizational dynamics rarely documented on the record: ideological infiltration attempts by the peace movement and Trotskyites, accusations of communism deployed against CORE chapters, and the personal toll Brown describes as comparable to post-traumatic stress.

Brown's post-movement trajectory is itself a primary-source account of how civil rights leadership translated into institution-building: founding the African American Studies program at San Diego State (1971), the Black Economic Development Task Force (1987), and the Community Leaders Undoing Biases (CLUB) group. His insistence that economic self-determination and capital formation constitute the unfinished work of the civil rights movement contributes to the historical record on the post-1965 transition from civil rights to economic-justice organizing.

chapters.title: York Pennsylvania Childhood
chapters.summary: Brown describes growing up in York, Pennsylvania in a segregated colored community where he encountered chain-works factory workers hurling racial insults on his walk to elementary school. He attended segregated schools through sixth grade. He cites his junior high homeroom teacher Cassandra Shea and a football coach as early figures who introduced him to college and professional aspirations.

chapters.title: Athletics and College Path
chapters.summary: Brown recounts his selection as Pennsylvania all-state basketball player, a brief failed tryout with the St. Louis Browns minor league in Thomasville, Georgia, and his Penn State basketball scholarship that paid only tuition. He explains how two Navy veterans from York who played at San Diego State encouraged him to transfer west.

chapters.title: Army Service
chapters.summary: Brown describes being drafted at the end of the Korean War, basic training at Camp Gordon in Georgia where he encountered signs reading 'no Jews or niggers allowed,' and permanent station at Fort Monmouth, NJ. He details his refused attempt to opt out of Operation Sagebrush in Louisiana and his brief visit to Shreveport, Louisiana.

chapters.title: San Diego State and Awakening
chapters.summary: Brown describes his time at San Diego State University as one of approximately 25 Black students out of 7,000 enrolled. As chair of the Student Council Constitutions Committee, he refused to approve white-only sorority and fraternity charters. He recounts being called a slur on the streets of San Diego and identifies anger as the catalyst for his decision to join the civil rights movement after graduation.

chapters.title: Teaching and Founding San Diego CORE
chapters.summary: Brown describes being denied a high school coaching position in La Mesa because the district would have to poll teachers about hiring a Negro. He joined the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee and was recruited around 1960 to help form the San Diego chapter of the Congress of Racial Equality. He also joined the Afro-American Association for Black history self-education, reading Lerone Bennett Jr. He became chapter chairman after his first year of teaching, having earned status as one of San Diego's five outstanding new teachers.

chapters.title: San Diego CORE Campaigns
chapters.summary: Brown details San Diego CORE's campaigns against employment discrimination, citing 19 segregated schools and grocery chains that refused to hire Black baggers or cashiers. He describes housing discrimination, including covenants and rental refusals affecting his future wife when she relocated from Ohio to work at General Dynamics in 1967. He recounts demonstrations against the California Real Estate Association and a previous Woolworths sit-in led by Dr. Jack Kimbrough.

chapters.title: Bank of America and SDG&E Campaigns
chapters.summary: As chairman of CORE's Western Region (California, Oregon, Washington, and Colorado), Brown led statewide direct action against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric to open employment to Black workers. Tactics included marches, sit-ins, mail-ins (mailing payments above or below balance owed), and coin-ins (paying in pennies at teller windows while others sang on the floor). Brown was jailed for trespassing, serving sentences on weekends while continuing to teach. He was summoned to Sacramento to defend his teaching credential.
related_events: CORE Western Region direct action campaigns

chapters.title: CORE Membership and Internal Conflict
chapters.summary: Brown notes that San Diego CORE membership was approximately two-thirds white during his tenure. He describes the difficulty of recruiting Black members, attributing fear of reprisals. He details internal conflict with the peace movement and Trotskyites attempting to influence CORE's direction, accusations of communism, and what he describes as an organized effort to remove him from leadership. He resigned in 1967.
related_events: FBI infiltration of civil rights organizations

chapters.title: Peace Corps in Lesotho
chapters.summary: Brown describes his year as Peace Corps deputy director in Lesotho, beginning with a layover in Johannesburg where he and his wife were confined to a Blacks-only room. He recounts King Moshoeshoe II's house arrest for opposing apartheid, Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan's accommodation with the regime, and the surprise of Basotho leaders when white Peace Corps volunteers arrived. He held a memorial service at an AME church in Lesotho after Martin Luther King's assassination, attended by King Moshoeshoe II. Brown returned to the United States following King's death.

chapters.title: Floyd McKissick Enterprises and Return
chapters.summary: Unable to find a position in San Diego after Peace Corps service, Brown worked with Floyd McKissick at McKissick Enterprises in Harlem and then at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street as a loan officer. He returned to San Diego in September 1971 when an old acquaintance recruited him as assistant to the president of San Diego State to organize what became the African American Studies program.

chapters.title: African American Studies and Administrative Career
chapters.summary: Brown became San Diego State's first Black administrator, hired faculty, and established a major and minor in what is now Africana Studies, securing university approval for the courses to fulfill general education requirements. He later directed campus computer services, then served 26 years as associate dean of the College of Business Administration, founding the Community Economic Development certificate program before retiring in 2004 to pursue real estate development.

chapters.title: Black Economic Development and Reflections
chapters.summary: Brown founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 and Community Leaders Undoing Biases (CLUB) to address what he identifies as the unfinished work of the civil rights movement: economic self-determination and capital formation. He critiques the movement's omission of economic education, notes Tony Brown's KPBS programming on Black business, and reflects on what he describes as post-traumatic stress carried by civil rights veterans. He calls for Black studies to be marketed across the university curriculum.

loc_video.caption: Harold K. Brown oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in San Diego, California, 2016 June 28
